perturbation.original_text: FCC agrees to provide $5,000,000 of commercial liability insurance in support of this indemnity which names Flotek as additional insured, with waiver of subrogation.
perturbation.changed_text: FCC agrees to provide commercial liability insurance in support of this indemnity.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific insurance amount and the waiver of subrogation weakens the protection for Flotek and may violate state-specific insurance requirements for commercial agreements. The original clause provides a specific coverage amount and ensures that the insurance company cannot sue Flotek to recover claim payments.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires specific disclosures and terms in insurance contracts to protect the insured. By omitting the specific coverage amount and waiver of subrogation, the contract fails to ensure adequate protection and potentially conflicts with the intent of Delaware's insurance regulations. The insurance amount can be deemed as a material element of the contract. Also a waiver of subrogation can be deemed a material part of the contract and must be clear.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the specific insurance amount ($5,000,000) and the waiver of subrogation weakens the protection for Flotek. The Law Explanation states this omission may violate state-specific insurance requirements and conflicts with the intent of Delaware's insurance regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines a 'policy' as 'the written contract of or written agreement for or effecting insurance, by whatever name called, and includes all clauses, riders, endorsements and papers which are a part thereof.' The insurance amount can be considered a material element, and the omission of a waiver of subrogation can be a significant change in the overall protection offered under the policy, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: FCC warrants to Flotek that: (a) all of the Terpene Product supplied by FCC to Flotek shall: (i) conform to the specifications set forth in Section 1; 3 (ii) comply with all relevant laws and regulations including, without limitation, laws and regulations of each of the jurisdictions in which the Terpene Product are either manufactured or to be sold or used concerning purity, sanitation, safety, security, and packaging and labeling of food and beverage; (iii) be in good condition at the time of delivery in all respects; and (iv) be free from any defect in design, workmanship, materials and packaging; and (b) it shall convey to Flotek good title to the Terpene Product free of any encumbrance, lien or security interest;
perturbation.changed_text: FCC warrants to Flotek that: (a) all of the Terpene Product supplied by FCC to Flotek shall: (i) conform to the specifications set forth in Section 1; (iii) be in good condition at the time of delivery in all respects; and (b) it shall convey to Flotek good title to the Terpene Product free of any encumbrance, lien or security interest;
perturbation.explanation: By removing the warranty that the Terpene Product will comply with all relevant laws and regulations concerning purity, sanitation, safety, security, and packaging and labeling, the contract potentially violates consumer protection laws. This is because the Terpene Product must still adhere to regulations such as those set by the FDA, even if the contract doesn't explicitly state so. The removal of that guarantee exposes Flotek to liability if FCC fails to comply with these laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: This law outlines prohibited acts regarding food, drugs, and cosmetics. Omitting the warranty of compliance allows FCC to potentially violate these regulations without contractual repercussions, putting Flotek at risk of legal consequences related to adulterated or misbranded products.

Prohibited acts The following acts and the causing thereof are prohibited: (a) The introduction or delivery for introduction into interstate commerce of any food, drug, device, tobacco product, or cosmetic that is adulterated or misbranded. (b) The adulteration or misbranding of any food, drug, device, tobacco product, or cosmetic in interstate commerce. (c) The receipt in in
perturbation.justification: The modified contract omits the warranty that the Terpene Product will comply with all relevant laws and regulations, specifically concerning purity, sanitation, safety, security, and packaging and labeling. This omission directly contradicts the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act, as indicated by 21 USC 331(a), which prohibits the introduction or delivery of any food, drug, or cosmetic that is adulterated or misbranded into interstate commerce. By removing the warranty of compliance, the contract creates a situation where FCC could potentially violate these regulations without contractual repercussions, thereby undermining consumer protection and potentially exposing Flotek to legal consequences.

perturbation.original_text: In addition, FCC shall be responsible for claims by third parties against Flotek for loss or damage based on personal injury or destruction of property due solely to defects in Terpene Product. FCC shall be responsible for the defense, settlement or other final disposition of such claims and agrees to hold Flotek harmless from any expenses or liability arising out of such claims. Flotek may, at its option and expense, retain counsel to participate in the investigation and handling of such claims, although FCC shall have control of all such claims, and the Flotek shall not settle or otherwise dispose of any such claims without the written consent of the FCC.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition, FCC shall be responsible for claims by third parties against Flotek for loss or damage based on personal injury or destruction of property due solely to defects in Terpene Product. FCC shall be responsible for the defense, settlement or other final disposition of such claims and agrees to hold Flotek harmless from any expenses or liability arising out of such claims. FCC shall have control of all such claims, and the Flotek shall not settle or otherwise dispose of any such claims without the written consent of the FCC.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'Flotek may, at its option and expense, retain counsel to participate in the investigation and handling of such claims' removes Flotek's right to legal representation in claims against them. Delaware law permits a party to seek independent legal counsel in such cases. This can violate Flotek's rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Lawyers' Rules of Professional Conduct Rule 1.2
perturbation.law_citation: DE Rules of Prof. Conduct Rule 1.2
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 1.2 emphasizes client autonomy in representation. By preventing Flotek from retaining their own counsel, the contract infringes upon their right to control the scope and objectives of their legal defense, even if FCC is primarily responsible for the claim. This is because under Delaware law, a client has a right to retain counsel and consult regarding matters. The elimination of this allowance violates this principle.
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly allows Flotek to retain counsel at their own expense to participate in the investigation and handling of claims. The changed text removes this right. The Perturbation Explanation states that Delaware law permits a party to seek independent legal counsel in such cases. The Law Explanation also points out that the contract infringes upon Flotek's right to control the scope and objectives of their legal defense by preventing them from retaining their own counsel. This removal of the right to retain counsel directly contradicts the principle of client autonomy and representation, even if the FCC is primarily responsible for the claim. Therefore, the changed text creates a legal contradiction.
